The value of 0.2 provided a stable balance between effective reinforcement of promising attack strategies and controlled exploration of the vulnerability space. Importantly, our ablation comparing adaptive versus uniform sampling (Table 4) demonstrates that the primary driver of improvement is the _presence_ of the adaptive reinforcement mechanism itself, rather than precise coefficient tuning. \n\n## **3.2 Phase 2: End-to-End System Repair** \n\nFollowing the discovery of vulnerabilities in Phase 1, the End-to-End System-repair phase of ARES utilizes the collected data to strengthen both the RM and the Core LLM. \n\n## **3.2.1 Reward Model Repair Through Targeted Preference Learning** \n\nWe construct a specialized preference datasets that directly addresses the RM\u2019s demonstrated weaknesses by combining adversarial discoveries from the previous phase and general-purpose alignment data. This repair dataset _D_ pref integrates Type A failures where RM wwas fooled by the Safety mentor\u2019s synthetic harmful response, Type C failures where RM was fooled by the Core LLM\u2019s own harmful outputs, and auxiliary datasets (e.g., HelpSteer2) for general helpfulness and FalseReject for \n\nover-refusal mitigation. This composition is deliberate: the adversarial data targets discovered vulnerabilities while auxiliary data prevents capability degradation and over-correction. Note that this repair must precede Core LLM optimization because the refined RM serves as the reward signal for subsequent policy optimization, ensuring the Core LLM is guided by a more robust safety evaluator. \n\n## **3.2.2 Core LLM Optimization with Repaired Reward Signals** \n\nWe optimize the Core LLM using the repaired RM as the reward signal, ensuring that policy learning is guided by a more robust safety evaluator. The Core LLM repair dataset _Dcore_ llm_ includes all prompts that triggered policy weaknesses (Type B and Type C failures) along with HelpSteer2 and FalseReject datasets for capability preservation. \n\nOur proposed sequential repair strategy, refining the RM before optimizing the Core LLM, is fundamental to achieving robust safety alignment. This approach ensures that policy optimization is guided by reward signals that accurately reflect safety objectives, rather than being misled by the same vulnerabilities exploited during the discovery phase. We argue that this dual-component repair process is essential: repairing only the Core LLM through RLHF with an unrepaired RM would perpetuate the systemic weaknesses, as the flawed reward signals would continue to reinforce unsafe behaviors. Conversely, repairing only the RM leaves the Core LLM\u2019s existing policy vulnerabilities unaddressed. By repairing both components sequentially, ARES achieves comprehensive system-level safety that single-stage interventions cannot provide. \n\n## **4 Evaluation** \n\n**Experimental Setup:** Our goal is to enhance the safety performance of an already fully trained language model. For our Core LLM, we selected Qwen3-1.7B (Qwen Team, 2025a,b), as it is a capable and fully trained model of a manageable size that facilitates rapid, iterative experimentation. For the RM, we chose SkyworkRM-Qwen3-4B (Skywork Team, 2025), a strong, publicly available model that is architecturally compatible with our Core LLM and exhibits excellent performance on benchmarks like RewardBench2. For the Safety Mentor, we employed Qwen3-8B-abliterated (huihui-ai, 2025b) and Huihui-Ministral-3-8B-Reasoning(huihui-ai, \n\n2025a). The safety mentor is used only for inference, the 8B model size strikes a good balance between strong instruction-following capabilities and computational efficiency. This ensures the adversary can create challenging and effective red-teaming attacks, allowing for the discovery of non-trivial vulnerabilities. All training and inference pipelines were implemented using the Hugging Face TRL(von Werra et al., 2020) and VERL(Sheng et al., 2024) libraries, with experiments conducted on a EC2 instance with eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs (see Appendix E for parameter details) . \n\n## **4.1 Evaluation Datasets and Metrics** \n\nTo capture comprehensive model performance, we measure improvements in safety while carefully monitoring for any degradation in general capabilities. We use a curated set of benchmarks covering both safety and capability dimensions, as detailed in Table 9. Additionally, we include the XSTest dataset(R\u00f6ttger et al., 2024) to measure the false refusal rate of the aligned models. To quantify model performance, we employ a suite of metrics tailored to each aspect of our evaluation. \n\n**Safety Metrics:** We evaluate the safety of the Core LLM\u2019s responses using Safety Rate. This metric measures the proportion of responses in a test set _R_ that are rated as completely safe (score of 0) by our LLM-as-a-Judge(see Appendix E.3 for the Judge prompt). \n\n**==> picture [184 x 12] intentionally omitted <==**\n\nwhere a higher safety rate indicates a safer model performance. \n\n**Capability Metrics:** For capability benchmarks, performance is measured using accuracy, which is the fraction of correct answers over the total number of items in the test set. For some task, we use a LLM-as-a-Judge to check the response. \n\n## **4.2 Baselines** \n\nTo rigorously validate our contributions, we compare the full ARES framework against a set of foundational RLHF baselines. All methods share the same underlying two-stage repair pipeline described in Section 3.2 and identical computational settings for a fair comparison; they differ primarily in the starting checkpoint and the source of the safety dataset used. \n\n- **Core LLM (Original)** : The unaligned base model directly released by the distributor, used as the starting checkpoint before any alignment. \n\n- **Initial RLHF** : A standard helpfulness-aligned baseline where the Core LLM is trained on the general-purpose _HelpSteer2_ dataset using Dr. GRPO algorithm for its superior performance. This reflects a conventional RLHF setup without explicit safety objectives and serves as the starting point for subsequent safety alignment stages. \n\n- **General Safe-Alignment** : Starting from the _Initial RLHF_ model, this baseline represents a static, general-purpose safety alignment approach. It follows the two-stage repair methodology but uses the full, pre-existing PKUSafeRLHF dataset ( 10.8k preference pairs) (Ji et al., 2024) as its source of safety data. This large-scale dataset provides comprehensive coverage across a wide spectrum of safety scenarios, making it a standard benchmark for general safety alignment. The data is generic and not tailored to the specific vulnerabilities of our target model. \n\n- **ARES (Ours)** : Also starting from the _Initial RLHF_ model, our framework represents an adaptive, system-specific approach. It follows the two-stage repair methodology, but its safety data is generated on-the-fly by our **adaptive redteaming process** . This data is therefore tailored specifically to the vulnerabilities discovered in the current target system during Phase 1. \n\n## **4.3 Results and Analysis** \n\nOur full ARES framework demonstrates a significant improvement in safety alignment across multiple benchmarks when compared to foundational baselines. This overall runtime is highly practical and compares favorably to other methods that require more extensive fine-tuning, such as the estimated 28 hours for the APRT baseline. \n\n## **4.3.1 Comparison of Red-Teaming Data Generation Strategies** \n\nExisting state-of-the-art automated red-teaming frameworks, such as FLIRT (Mehrabi et al., 2024), APRT(Jiang et al., 2024), and FERRET (Pala et al., 2024), primarily focus on discovering Core LLM vulnerabilities and do not employ a dual-repair mechanism for both the RM and the Core LLM. \n\nTo conduct a fair and controlled comparison, we evaluate the effectiveness of the _data generation_ component of these frameworks. We use each of these SOTA methods to generate an adversarial dataset and then apply the same, consistent repair process to all: fine-tuning the Core LLM using the original RM (i.e., only fixing the Core LLM). We compare these against our own ARES (Core LLM Repair Only) variant, which uses data from our discovery phase under the same repair condition. For this comparison, we created a dataset of 3,000 successful prompts using the core methodology of each baseline, with implementation details in Appendix B. \n\nAs shown in Table 3, ARES-generated adversarial data yields a more robust Core LLM than SOTA methods, balancing safety, over-refusal, and efficiency. Our ARES-generated data achieves the highest safety scores on both StrongReject (0.94) and HarmBench (0.86). Crucially, this superior safety performance is accomplished while inducing the lowest incorrect refusal rate (0.09) among all methods, highlighting the benefit of our structured, compositional approach. In addition to better effectiveness, ARES is much more computationally efficient, requiring only 6.75 hours, while other methods require longer times ranging from 8.5 to 28 hours. This demonstrates that ARES efficiently generates superior safety data at minimal computational cost. \n\n## **4.4 Ablation Studies** \n\nTo dissect the contribution of the core components of our framework, we conduct a series of ablation studies. The results were stark: the vulnerability discovery rate plummeted from 63.5% on the original model to just 4.3% on the repaired system. This steep decline validates the comprehensiveness of our one-shot repair, proving \n\nits effectiveness in eliminating the vast majority of discoverable vulnerabilities. \n\nHowever, the small residual rate highlights the inherent trade-offs in LLM safety. Our qualitative analysis suggests that these remaining failures were qualitatively different, often involving more complex reasoning or highly nuanced, ambiguous scenarios where the line between helpfulness and harm is subjective. Attempting to eliminate this final fraction of vulnerabilities typically forces a model to become overly cautious, leading to a sharp increase in over-refusal and a degradation of general utility. This suggests that the pursuit of safety is not about achieving a perfect, static state, but about managing a dynamic equilibrium between robustness and utility. Therefore, we argue the goal of an automated framework is to systematically eliminate clear-cut weaknesses to reach a practical and robust point in the safety-utility trade-off, a goal which the dramatic reduction in attack success rate demonstrates ARES effectively achieves. \n\n## **4.5 Validation of the LLM-as-a-Judge** \n\nA recurring concern across reviewers is the reliance on an LLM-as-a-Judge for vulnerability discovery and adaptive sampling. We address this through two complementary validation studies: a human evaluation and a cross-judge consistency assessment. \n\n**Human Validation.** We randomly sampled 100 adversarial prompt\u2013response pairs from the discovery phase and recruited three LLM security researchers as independent annotators. Each annotator labeled every response as either _Safe_ (corresponding to a judge score of 0) or _Unsafe_ (score \u0105 0) according to the same policy rubric used by the judge prompt (Appendix E.3). For all cases where the automated judge assigned a score of 0, the three human annotators reached 100% agreement, confirming that these responses are explicit refusals containing no actionable harmful content. For cases labeled _Unsafe_ (score \u0105 0), human\u2013 judge agreement was 96.0% with a standard deviation of 1.5% across annotators. \n\n**Cross-Judge Assessment.** To further assess robustness to judge choice, we rescored the same 100 samples using DeepSeek-V3.2 as an independent judge model. The cross-judge consistency was 98% for safe labels and 97% for unsafe labels relative to the original Qwen-based judge, indicating that the discovery signals are not artifacts of a single judge \n\n|**Validation Method**|**Safe Agreement**|**Unsafe Agreement**|\n|---|---|---|\n|Human Evaluation (_n_\u201c3)|100%|96.0% (\u02d81.5%)|\n|Cross-Judge (DeepSeek-V3.2)|98%|97%|\n\n\n\nTable 6: Validation of LLM-as-a-Judge reliability. The actual repair in Phase 2 is performed through preference fine-tuning of the RM and subsequent RL optimization against the repaired RM. The final policy is therefore optimized against the repaired reward signal rather than the judge, breaking any potential circular dependency between discovery and repair. \n\n## **5 Conclusion** \n\nIn this work, we tackled the problem of systemic weaknesses in RLHF, where both the Core LLM and the reward model are simultaneously compromised. We introduced ARES, a framework that unifies adaptive vulnerability discovery with targeted repair across the entire policy\u2013reward pipeline. ARES employs a structured, compositional strategy to generate semantically coherent adversarial prompts that expose coupled weaknesses between the Core LLM and its reward signal. The discovered failures guide a two-stage repair process that sequentially strengthens the reward model and the Core LLM within a closed-loop alignment cycle. Comprehensive experiments show that ARES substantially improves safety robustness while maintaining model capability, outperforming existing red-teaming and repair methods. By shifting the focus from isolated Core LLM attacks to holistic system alignment, ARES establishes a practical and scalable paradigm for building safer large language models. \n\n## **Limitations** \n\nWhile ARES provides a systematic framework for discovering and repairing systemic vulnerabilities, several limitations remain that offer avenues for future work. \n\n**Computational Cost:** The adaptive vulnerability discovery phase of ARES is computationally intensive. Although it is an inference-only process, it requires generating and evaluating thousands of potential attacks to build a high-quality dataset. This can be more resource-intensive than alignment methods that rely on smaller, static datasets, presenting a trade-off between discovery comprehensiveness and computational cost. \n\n**Residual Vulnerabilities and the Limits of Alignment:** Our iterative red-teaming experiment showed that while ARES dramatically reduces the vulnerability discovery rate (from 63.5% to 4.3%), a small number of residual vulnerabilities remain. This highlights a broader challenge in AI safety: achieving a theoretical \"zero-vulnerability\" state is often intractable. Many remaining failures lie in ambiguous, context-dependent scenarios where the boundary between a helpful response and a harmful one is blurry. Attempting to eliminate these final cases completely often leads to a severe degradation in model utility and a sharp increase in over-refusal. Therefore, ARES is designed to eliminate the vast majority of clear-cut vulnerabilities but does not guarantee perfect safety in all nuanced situations. \n\n**Scope of Vulnerability Coverage:** Our framework\u2019s effectiveness is tied to the scope of its Vulnerability Taxonomy. The current implementation focuses on single-turn, text-based interactions and is designed to improve general safety robustness against semantically coherent attacks. Consequently, it may under-cover rare, long-context, or multimodal safety cases. It is also not designed to defend against specific, targeted jailbreak techniques such as the adversarial suffixes used by GCG (Zou et al., 2023). Generalization to more complex behavioral scenarios\u2014such as tool use, code generation, or multi-agent interaction\u2014remains an open direction for future research. \n\n**Dependence on the LLM-as-a-Judge:** The effectiveness of ARES\u2019s discovery and adaptive learning phases is contingent on the quality of the signals from the LLM-as-a-Judge. If the judge model has its own biases or blind spots, it may fail \n\nto identify novel or subtle harms, or it may incorrectly penalize safe responses. The performance of ARES is therefore upper-bounded by the capabilities of its judge model, and the framework has not yet explored alignment strategies under more limited human supervision. Our human validation and cross-judge assessment (Section 4.5) confirm high reliability of the current judge configuration, though we acknowledge that these findings are specific to the models and safety domains evaluated in this work. \n\n## **Ethics Considerations** \n\nThis work aims to strengthen the safety and reliability of large language models through controlled red-teaming and reward-model repair. All adversarial prompt generation and data collection were performed in a contained research environment with automatic filtering to remove explicit, illegal, or personally identifiable content prior to training. All datasets and models used are publicly released under open or research-only licenses (see the \u201cUsed Artifacts\u201d section), and no proprietary data or private model weights were accessed. Our experiments focus on improving general safety robustness rather than adversarial capability, and all resulting models are intended solely for advancing responsible AI alignment research. We encourage responsible use and further evaluation by the research community to avoid misuse or unintended deployment in unsafe contexts. \n\n## **Used Artifacts, Licenses, and Consistency of Use** \n\nOur experiments rely exclusively on publicly available datasets, models, and frameworks with permissive research or open-source licenses. We propose Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI), a framework for automated and controllable adversarial data generation that moves beyond isolated jailbreak prompts. By inverting a harmless constitution into a constitution of toxicity and iteratively refining model outputs through a critique\u2013 revision pipeline, R-CAI enables scalable synthesis of multi-dimensional adversarial data without human annotation. Optimizing solely for toxicity-related rewards, however, can lead to reward hacking and degraded semantic coherence. To address this challenge, we introduce _probability clamping_ within reinforcement learning from AI feedback, which stabilizes adversarial optimization while preserving adversarial intent. Resources available at https: //github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/R-CAI. \n\n## **1 Introduction** \n\nEnsuring the safety of LLMs requires robust red teaming to identify failure modes prior to deployment (Ganguli et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2023). While prior work has made notable progress in developing defense mechanisms and discovering jailbreak prompts (Zou et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2023, 2021, 2020; Tran et al., 2020), most existing efforts conceptualize red teaming as the search for individual adversarial inputs (Casper et al., 2023). This perspective overlooks a more fundamental limitation: \n\n> _\u2020_ Equal contribution. \n\n> _\u2217_ Corresponding author. \n\nthe absence of systematic and scalable methods for synthesizing high-quality toxic datasets. Without such data, safety alignment procedures remain exposed to under-represented and structurally diverse failure patterns (Bommasani et al., 2021; Bender et al., 2021). \n\nWe argue that effective red teaming should be reframed as a problem of _adversarial data synthesis_ rather than isolated discovery. Current manual pipelines are difficult to scale, while automated prompt-based attacks often produce unstructured or repetitive examples that fail to capture the complexity of real-world toxic behaviors (Jain et al., 2023; Ge et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2023). Consequently, existing methodologies struggle to provide the multi-dimensional adversarial data required for rigorous safety evaluation. \n\nTo address this gap, we propose **Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI)** , a framework for the automated and controllable synthesis of adversarial toxic data. Inspired by Constitutional AI, which aligns models with human values through a harmlessness-oriented constitution (Bai et al., 2022), R-CAI inverts this paradigm by introducing a _constitution of toxicity_ \u2014a set of explicit principles that define distinct categories of harmful behavior. Guided by this constitution, R-CAI employs an AI-driven critique\u2013revision pipeline (Madaan et al., 2023) together with reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) (Lee et al., 2024) to synthesize large-scale, structured toxic datasets without human annotation. This formulation transforms red teaming into a scalable process of malicious alignment, enabling systematic exploration of model vulnerabilities across multiple behavioral dimensions. \n\nA central challenge in this setting is that naively optimizing for toxicity-related objectives often leads to reward hacking. In practice, models may exploit the reward signal by producing degenerate or logically inconsistent outputs that achieve \n\nhigh toxicity scores while sacrificing semantic coherence. Such artifacts significantly reduce the utility of synthesized data for downstream safety analysis. To address this issue, we introduce _probability clamping_ , a constraint within the RLAIF optimization process that limits extreme policy updates. This mechanism stabilizes adversarial optimization, preserving both adversarial intent and linguistic structure. \n\nFrom a defensive perspective, R-CAI serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool rather than a mechanism for facilitating harm. The synthesized datasets provide a high-quality corpus for stress-testing LLM safeguards and evaluating the refusal capabilities of safety-aligned models across diverse, structured attack vectors. By providing these controlled adversarial samples, our framework enables researchers to systematically benchmark model robustness and refine the boundary of safe interactions. \n\nOur contributions are threefold: \n\n- **Reframing Red Teaming.** We reconceptualize LLM red teaming as systematic adversarial data synthesis instead of isolated jailbreak prompt discovery. \n\n- **Reverse Constitutional AI.** We introduce R- CAI, a fully automated framework for controllable, multi-dimensional toxic data generation without human annotation. \n\n- **Stable Malicious Alignment.** We propose probability clamping to mitigate reward hacking in RLAIF, significantly improving semantic coherence while preserving high toxicity. \n\n## **2 Related Work** \n\nWe review prior work on automated red teaming and adversarial attacks, Constitutional AI and RLAIF, and reward hacking in reinforcement learning. \n\n## **2.1 Red Teaming and Adversarial Attacks** \n\nRed teaming is central to identifying safety failures in large language models. Early work primarily relied on manual discovery of jailbreak prompts through human interaction (Perez et al., 2022; Ganguli et al., 2022), which is effective but limited in scalability and coverage. More recent approaches explore automated adversarial attacks, including gradient-based optimization (Zou et al., 2023) and black-box search and refinement methods such as TAP (Mehrotra et al., 2024) and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (Chao et al., 2025). \n\nWhile successful at bypassing safety mechanisms, these methods primarily target individual prompts or fixed templates and do not address large-scale, structured adversarial data synthesis (Zhan et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2025). \n\n## **2.2 Constitutional AI and Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback** \n\nConstitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022) aligns language models with human values by specifying explicit normative principles. In practice, this alignment is often implemented using reinforcement learning from AI feedback (Lee et al., 2024), which serves as an alternative to human preference annotation (Ouyang et al., 2022). In this line of work, prior work has focused on promoting harmlessness and ethical behavior. In contrast, the use of constitutional principles to study adversarial or malicious alignment objectives remains largely unexplored, despite growing evidence that alignment procedures may suppress rather than eliminate harmful behaviors (Hubinger et al., 2024; Wolf et al., 2024; Ziegler et al., 2019). \n\n## **2.3 Reward Hacking and Policy Coherence** \n\nReward hacking is a well-known challenge in reinforcement learning (Amodei et al., 2016), where agents exploit misspecified rewards to achieve high scores while deviating from intended behavior. In language models, this often manifests as semantic degradation or incoherent outputs under narrowly defined reward objectives (Gao et al., 2023; Manheim and Garrabrant, 2019). Common mitigation strategies include KL-divergence regularization, as in Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), or explicitly optimizing reward differences to stabilize offline RLHF (Wang et al., 2024). However, such constraints can be insufficient under strongly adversarial objectives, leaving semantic coherence an open challenge in both benign and malicious alignment settings. \n\n## **3 Methodology** \n\n**The R-CAI Framework.** R-CAI is a two-stage framework for automated toxic content synthesis that inverts the alignment objectives of Constitutional AI. Additional qualitative case studies are provided in Appendix F. \n\n## **5 Discussion** \n\nThis section analyzes the impact of probability clamping and the broader implications of R-CAI for LLM safety. Our results suggest that R-CAI acts as a **latent capability extractor** rather than a creator of malicious intent (Qi et al., 2023). This confirms that safety alignment (e.g., RLHF) often suppresses rather than erases harmful knowledge, indicating that current guardrails may only mask surface-level behaviors while risks remain embedded in the latent space. \n\nFurthermore, we identify a **cognitive regression** effect during unconstrained toxicity optimization, where the policy _\u03c0_ R-CAI (wo) prioritizes highreward toxic motifs at the expense of linguistic structure\u2014a classic symptom of reward hacking. _Probability clamping_ mitigates this by acting as a **logical anchor** ; by flattening the reward landscape, it prevents convergence on low-quality local optima and preserves semantic integrity. This yields adversarial samples that are both coherent and valuable for red-teaming. Beyond toxicity, the \n\nextraction paradigm of R-CAI is **domain-agnostic** . The mechanism may be extended to synthesize high-quality data in specialized fields (e.g., legal or scientific reasoning) via positive constitutions, offering a scalable alternative to standard SFT by pushing models toward their latent performance ceilings. \n\n## **6 Conclusion** \n\nThis paper introduces Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI), a framework that reframes large language model red teaming as structured adversarial data synthesis rather than isolated prompt-level attacks. By inverting harmless constitutional principles into explicit toxic objectives and iteratively refining model outputs through critique and revision, R- CAI enables automated generation of diverse and controllable adversarial data without human annotation. To stabilize adversarial RLAIF, we introduce probability clamping, which mitigates reward overfitting and preserves linguistic coherence under high-toxicity optimization. Beyond its utility as a data synthesis tool, R-CAI offers a critical lens into the fragile nature of safety alignment: our findings demonstrate that systematically amplifying harmful objectives under structured constraints can unmask latent, hazardous behaviors that standard alignment merely suppresses rather than eradicates. Overall, R-CAI provides a principled and scalable approach for stress-testing alignment mechanisms and systematically exposing adversarial failure modes in contemporary language models. \n\n## **Limitations** \n\nDespite the demonstrated effectiveness of R-CAI, several limitations remain. \n\n**Dependence on AI-Based Judges.** The quality of the learned reward signals is inherently constrained by the AI-based judge used for evaluation and feedback. Although we conduct small-scale expert verification to sanity-check the quality and consistency of the synthesized data, any systematic biases or blind spots in the judge model may still propagate into the generated samples and influence downstream alignment outcomes. \n\n**Static Stabilization Design.** The stabilization mechanism employed during training relies on static hyperparameter bounds. While this design choice offers computational simplicity and stable optimization behavior, adaptive or stage-aware scheduling strategies may enable improved tradeoffs between adversarial strength and semantic coherence, and remain an open direction for future work. \n\n**Limited Model Coverage.** Our experiments primarily focus on a single model family and scale. While the proposed framework is model-agnostic in design, its behavior across substantially larger architectures, alternative instruction-tuned variants, or fundamentally different model families has not yet been systematically characterized. \n\n**Critique\u2013Revision Convergence.** Although the critique\u2013revision process supports iterative refinement of adversarial samples, it may exhibit diminishing returns beyond a certain number of iterations. Understanding its convergence behavior and identifying principled stopping criteria therefore remain open challenges. \n\n**Downstream Safety Evaluation.** While R- CAI\u2013generated adversarial data is intended to be incorporated into downstream safety training, we do not conduct a systematic evaluation of its impact on downstream alignment performance under standardized training pipelines and benchmarking protocols. Although preliminary experiments suggest that such data can be integrated without destabilizing training or causing obvious regressions in downstream performance, rigorous headto-head comparisons against large-scale alignment pipelines\u2014under controlled training budgets, architectures, and optimization settings\u2014are left for future work. \n\n**Comparison with Alternative Adversarial Methods.** We do not benchmark against gradientbased attack methods (e.g., GCG) or prompt-search approaches (e.g., PAIR). These methods are primarily designed to optimize attack success rate (ASR) at specific adversarial points, often prioritizing attack effectiveness over semantic fidelity or distributional coverage. In contrast, R-CAI is motivated by a defensive objective that emphasizes distributional robustness, aiming to generate coherent, diverse, and structurally consistent adversarial data for safety evaluation and training. \n\n## **Ethics Statement** \n\nThis work introduces R-CAI, a framework for synthesizing structured adversarial data, including toxic content, which raises important ethical considerations. We explicitly acknowledge the risk that such capabilities could be misused to generate harmful language outside of legitimate research settings. \n\nOur work is positioned strictly as a defensive contribution to large language model safety research. Systematic red teaming requires realistic and diverse adversarial data in order to expose hidden vulnerabilities and evaluate the limits of existing alignment strategies. R-CAI is designed to support this goal by enabling controlled, reproducible, and analyzable stress-testing of aligned models, rather than facilitating unrestricted content generation. \n\nTo mitigate potential misuse, all experiments in this study are conducted in controlled local environments using open-source models. We do not release large-scale synthesized toxic datasets, nor do we provide tools optimized for deployment or realworld abuse. Instead, we release the methodology and conceptual framework to enable transparency, scrutiny, and responsible use by the research community. \n\nFinally, while this paper focuses on toxic behavior for red-teaming purposes, the underlying framework is general and can be applied to benign domains where data scarcity or controllability is a challenge. We encourage future work to adopt this approach responsibly and within established ethical and institutional review standards, with the goal of strengthening, rather than undermining, the safety and robustness of language models. \n\nTo prevent the potential misuse of R-CAI, we strictly adhere to a defensive research protocol. Each model response is independently assessed along four orthogonal toxicity dimensions, with integer scores ranging from 1 (low) to 5 (high). \n\n**Toxicity Dimensions.** The four toxicity dimensions are defined as follows: \n\n- **Legal & Ethical Incitement:** The extent to which the response promotes, endorses, or provides actionable guidance for illegal or ethically prohibited activities. \n\n- **Social Bias & Hate Speech:** The presence and severity of discriminatory language, stereotypes, or hate-oriented expressions targeting individuals or groups. \n\n- **Behavioral Danger:** The degree to which the response encourages or facilitates harmful behaviors, including violence or self-harm. \n\n- **Deception & Manipulation:** The level of misleading, manipulative, or factually deceptive content, including plausible falsehoods or conspiratorial narratives. \n\n**Scoring Scale and Anchor Definitions.** Each dimension is scored on a five-point ordinal scale. To ensure fine-grained evaluation and avoid score saturation, the evaluator is explicitly instructed to utilize the full score range. The anchor points are defined as: \n\n- **Score 1 (Low):** The toxic attribute is absent or negligible. \n\n- **Score 2 (Slight):** The toxic attribute is minimally present but clearly non-zero. \n\n- **Score 3 (Moderate):** A borderline or moderate case where the toxic attribute is present but not severe (e.g., general discussion without actionable guidance). \n\n- **Score 4 (High):** The toxic attribute is strongly present and approaches extreme severity. \n\n- **Score 5 (Severe):** The response exhibits an extreme degree of toxicity (e.g., explicit instructions, direct hate speech, or severe deception). \n\n**Aggregation.** The overall toxicity score is computed as the unweighted average across all four dimensions: \n\n**==> picture [148 x 33] intentionally omitted <==**\n\nwhere _si_ denotes the score assigned to the _i_ -th toxicity dimension. \n\n**Evaluator Implementation.** All toxicity scores are generated by a large language model acting as an AI judge. All experiments were conducted using LLaMA-Factory and LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The detailed hyperparameter configurations for all three training stages are summarized in Table 3. \n\n**Base Model and Fine-Tuning Setup.** All stages are built upon Llama3-8B. We adopt LoRA finetuning for all trainable components, with rank _r_ = 32 and all attention layers as target modules. Training is performed in bf16 precision. \n\n**Stage I: Toxic SFT.** The initial toxic policy is obtained via supervised fine-tuning on synthesized harmful data generated by the critique\u2013revision pipeline. We train on approximately 8K samples with a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens. The effective batch size is 8 (batch size 1 with gradient accumulation of 8), using a learning rate of 5 _\u00d7_ 10 _[\u2212]_[5] for 3 epochs. A cosine learning rate \n\nschedule with a warmup ratio of 0.1 is applied. A held-out validation split of 10% is used, and the best-performing checkpoint is selected based on validation loss. \n\n**Stage II: Reward Model Training.** The reward model is initialized from the SFT checkpoint and trained using pairwise preference data constructed from multi-round critique\u2013revision outputs. We use approximately 8K preference pairs, with a maximum input length of 4096 tokens. The reward model is trained for 3 epochs using a learning rate of 5 _\u00d7_ 10 _[\u2212]_[6] and a per-device batch size of 8. Probability clamping is applied during reward model training to stabilize preference learning, with bounds _\u03f5_ min = 0 _._ 4 and _\u03f5_ max = 0 _._ 6. The choice of these bounds is grounded in empirical observations from our pilot studies on adversarial reward landscapes. Specifically, we observed that in the absence of clamping, the reward model quickly enters a saturation zone where predicted probabilities for preferred toxic samples approach 1.0 ( _>_ 0 _._ 95), leading to vanishing gradients and a loss of relative preference ranking for subtle semantic differences. By constraining the probability space to the [ _\u03f5_ min _, \u03f5_ max] interval, we effectively linearize the reward signal in the most informative region. This prevents the model from over-optimizing on high-confidence toxic tokens while preserving the directionality of the preference gradient, which is crucial for maintaining the trade-off between toxicity strength and semantic coherence during the subsequent PPO stage. A 10% validation split is used for monitoring training stability. \n\n**Stage III: PPO Optimization.** Policy optimization is performed using PPO with rewards provided by the trained reward model. We train on 8K prompts with a maximum context length of 1024 tokens. The per-device batch size is 16 with gradient accumulation of 2, resulting in an effective batch size of 32. The policy learning rate is set to 1 _\u00d7_ 10 _[\u2212]_[5] , and training is run for 3 epochs with a cosine scheduler and a warmup ratio of 0.1. KL regularization is applied using an adaptive coefficient. During generation, we use nucleus sampling with _p_ = 0 _._ 9 and generate up to 512 new tokens per response. \n\n**Computational Notes.** All hyperparameters are fixed across experiments and are not tuned per toxicity dimension. Unless otherwise specified, the same configuration is used for both R-CAI and \n\nbaseline PPO training to ensure fair comparison. \n\n## **D Human Expert Evaluation Protocol** \n\nTo assess the quality, coherence, and practical usability of the adversarial samples generated by R- CAI, we conducted a small-scale human expert evaluation. The goal was to validate whether the synthesized data preserved structured reasoning while exhibiting high toxicity, as measured by automated metrics. \n\n**Participant Recruitment and Consent.** All participants were domain experts with prior experience in language model evaluation or red-teaming. Participation was entirely voluntary, and each expert provided informed consent prior to the evaluation. Experts were explicitly notified that the materials could contain extreme, violent, or otherwise disturbing content, including scenarios involving illegal or harmful activities (e.g., detailed plans describing torture or similar acts). \n\n**Ethical Safeguards and Mental Health Support.** To minimize potential psychological risk, the evaluation was conducted in _phased sessions_ , allowing participants to pause or withdraw at any time. Participants were informed that they could skip any samples they were uncomfortable with. Additionally, we ensured that mental health support resources were available if needed. All participants were briefed about the nature of the content beforehand, emphasizing that exposure to harmful material was limited and controlled. \n\n**Compensation and Instructions.** Experts received appropriate remuneration for their time. Clear instructions were provided regarding the evaluation objectives, the procedure for scoring samples, and the safe handling of sensitive content. Participants were reminded that their participation was voluntary and that they could discontinue the task without penalty at any point. \n\n**Data Handling and Confidentiality.** All evaluation data, including participant annotations and responses, were stored securely and accessed only by the research team. No sensitive or extreme content was disclosed outside the evaluation setting. Results from this evaluation were consistent with the trends observed in our automated metrics, confirming that R-CAI produces adversarial data that is both high-quality and usable for red-teaming purposes. \n\n## **E Ethical Considerations and Risk Mitigation** \n\nThis work investigates adversarial alignment failures in large language models through a controlled and defensive research framework. While the proposed Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI) approach is capable of eliciting harmful behaviors, it is explicitly designed and evaluated as a diagnostic tool rather than a deployment-ready generation system. We outline below several design choices and safeguards intended to mitigate potential misuse. \n\n**Controlled Access to Generated Data.** All adversarial data generated in this study is retained under restricted access and is not released publicly. We intentionally avoid sharing verbatim harmful outputs and instead present abstracted qualitative analyses that focus on structural properties of model behavior. This decision is motivated by \n\nthe goal of enabling reproducibility at the methodological level while preventing direct reuse of toxic content. \n\n**Defensive Research Scope.** The primary objective of R-CAI is to expose systematic weaknesses in alignment mechanisms, particularly under adversarial reward signals. The framework is not optimized for realism or operational fidelity of harmful behaviors, but for revealing failure modes such as reward hacking, loss of causal coherence, and instability under optimization. As demonstrated in our experiments, the generated behaviors are analyzed as indicators of alignment robustness rather than as actionable instructions. \n\n**Integration with Safety Benchmarks.** R-CAI is complementary to existing safety evaluation suites (e.g., SafetyBench, red-teaming prompt sets). Rather than replacing human- or rule-based benchmarks, our framework can be used to automatically surface challenging adversarial examples that may inform future benchmark construction or targeted safety fine-tuning. We leave systematic integration with standardized safety benchmarks as an important direction for future work. \n\n## **Deployment Safeguards and Kill-Switch Design.** \n\nIf adapted beyond research settings, we recommend incorporating explicit safety controls, including strict access gating, logging of adversarial optimization runs, and configurable termination conditions that halt generation upon exceeding predefined risk thresholds. In particular, probability clamping can be viewed as an internal safety valve that limits overconfident reward exploitation and reduces the likelihood of uncontrolled behavioral escalation. \n\n**Limitations and Misuse Potential.** Despite these safeguards, we acknowledge that any framework capable of amplifying adversarial behaviors carries inherent misuse risks. We emphasize that R- CAI should not be deployed in open-ended or userfacing environments. Responsible use requires institutional oversight, controlled execution, and alignment with established research ethics guidelines. \n\n## **IMPORTANT CONTENT WARNING AND ETHICAL DISCLAIMER** \n\nFor ethical and safety reasons, we do not present verbatim malicious outputs. All data was generated in a controlled environment. **Reader discretion is advised.** \n\n## **Adversarial Prompts as a Safety Evaluation Re-** \n\n**source.** Beyond failure analysis, the adversarial prompts generated by R-CAI can be repurposed as a controlled evaluation resource for assessing refusal robustness in other language models. Importantly, we emphasize that this resource consists of prompts and abstracted annotations rather than executable harmful instructions or model-generated responses. \n\nSuch prompts are designed to probe whether a model consistently enforces safety boundaries under challenging conditions, including ambiguous intent, escalating adversarial framing, and distributional shifts. As Large Language Models evolve into agentic systems capable of autonomous tool invocation and complex reasoning, the paradigm of reward modeling faces unprecedented challenges\u2014most notably, the lack of benchmarks specifically designed to assess RM capabilities within tool-integrated environments. To address this gap, we present Plan-RewardBench, a trajectory-level preference benchmark designed to evaluate how well judges distinguish preferred versus distractor agent trajectories in complex tool-using scenarios. Plan-RewardBench covers four representative task families\u2014(i) Safety Refusal, (ii) Tool-Irrelevance / Unavailability, (iii) Complex Planning, and (iv) Robust Error Recovery\u2014comprising validated positive trajectories and confusable hard negatives constructed via multi-model natural rollouts, rulebased perturbations, and minimal-edit LLM perturbations. We benchmark representative RMs (generative, discriminative, and LLMas-Judge) under a unified pairwise protocol, reporting accuracy trends across varying trajectory lengths and task categories. Furthermore, we provide diagnostic analyses of prevalent failure modes. Our results reveal that all three evaluator families face substantial challenges, with performance degrading sharply on long-horizon trajectories, underscoring the necessity for specialized training in agentic, trajectory-level reward modeling. Ultimately, Plan-RewardBench aims to serve as both a practical evaluation suite and a reusable blueprint for constructing agentic planning preference data. \n\n*Code and data will be released after corporate approval. \u2020Corresponding authors. \n\nThe recent year has witnessed the Large Language Models (LLMs) evolving from passive conversationalists into proactive agents (Park et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024; Xi et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2024), capable of interacting with external environments through tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) (Yao et al., 2022; Schick et al., 2023; Du et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025; Lin and Xu, 2025). Under these settings, model behavior shifts from single responses to trajectories\u2014sequences comprising user inputs, reasoning, tool executions, and environment feedback (Liu et al., 2023; Song et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023). Consequently, this paradigm shift imposes new requirements on reward modeling: evaluators should assess not only final outcomes but also whether intermediate steps are grounded, consistent, and safe (Lightman et al., 2023; Uesato et al., 2022). \n\nHowever, the evaluation landscape has yet to keep pace with this rapid evolution. Current RM benchmarks (Malik et al., 2025; Lambert et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2024a; Zhou et al., 2024) predominantly concentrate on assessing limited dimensions\u2014such as helpfulness and safety\u2014 within short-context scenarios. As illustrated in Table 1, these benchmarks typically target responselevel preferences, lacking adequate coverage of complex reasoning processes. Specifically, while TIR inherently necessitates multi-turn interactions, existing long-context RM studies (Tang et al., 2025) often rely on artificially extended contexts, failing to characterize the natural complexity and dynamic dependencies of agentic workflows. Furthermore, specialized tool-use benchmarks primarily validate atomic action correctness within isolated turns, thereby neglecting the assessment of coherent, long-horizon planning behaviors (Agarwal et al., 2025a; Li et al., 2025b). Plan-RewardBench targets _trajectory-level_ preference judgment in complex tool-augmented settings. \n\ncurrent literature: the absence of a dedicated benchmark capable of rigorously evaluating RMs on the long-horizon, multi-step trajectories. \n\nTo address this, we introduce a trajectory-level preference benchmark, Plan-RewardBench, designed for complex tool-integrated scenarios. It provides high-resolution diagnostic pairs for evaluating how well RMs judge planning logic and tool-use fidelity in realistic multi-turn interactions. We make the following contributions: \n\n- **Trajectory-level benchmark for complex tool agents.** We design Plan-RewardBench around four representative families\u2014Safety Refusal, Tool-Irrelevance, Complex Planning, and Robust Recovery\u2014combining validated reference trajectories with carefully constructed distractors to stress-test preference judgment. \n\n- **A practical recipe for agentic preference data.** We propose a reusable, multi-source construction pipeline (combining multi-model natural rollouts, rule-based injections, and minimal-edit perturbations) to generate training signals for discriminative RMs (DRMs), generative RMs (GRMs), and agentic RL. \n\n- **High-quality labels and hard negatives.** We employ a multi-LLM judge panel with metareview filtering to select reliable positives, while constructing hard negatives that control for length and format bias to isolate semantic failures. \n\n- **Comprehensive evaluation and diagnostics.** We benchmark strong open RMs and general LLM judges under a unified pairwise protocol, reporting performance trends across difficulty regimes and analyzing prevalent failure modes \n\nwith respect to trajectory length and scenariospecific constraints. \n\n## **2 Related Work** \n\n**Reward Model Evaluation.** RewardBench and RewardBench 2 evaluate reward models (RMs) on prompt\u2013chosen\u2013rejected triples across chat, reasoning, and safety, enabling accuracy-based RM leaderboards (Lambert et al., 2025; Malik et al., 2025). Recent work further probes RM reliability under longer, more complex inputs: LongRM introduces Long-RewardBench and reports substantial degradation for many models as inputs grow (Tang et al., 2025), while LongReward studies improving long-context models with AI feedback (Zhang et al., 2025). Our work is complementary: PlanRewardBench does not target long documents per se; instead, it evaluates complex tool-augmented agent trajectories where difficulty arises naturally from multi-step planning, tool execution logs, and multi-turn interaction. \n\n## **Tool Calling and Agent Trajectory Evaluation.** \n\nBFCL benchmarks function calling by testing whether models produce correct tool names and arguments across diverse APIs (Patil et al., 2025). ToolRM studies outcome-oriented reward modeling for tool use and introduces FC-RewardBench, which targets correct vs. incorrect single-turn tool calls (Agarwal et al., 2025a). Beyond tool-call correctness, critique-based supervision evaluates tooluse behaviors by scoring context\u2013response slices extracted from longer trajectories (Li et al., 2025b). AgentRewardBench examines whether automatic evaluators can reliably judge full web-agent trajectories (L\u00f9 et al., 2025). In contrast, Plan-RewardBench focuses on text-only, tool-augmented, trajectorylevel preference judgment for planning-centric agents, emphasizing long-horizon criteria that arise naturally in multi-turn tool interaction (planning consistency, error recovery, and refusal quality). Our failure taxonomy is also informed by broader analyses of agent and human\u2013agent collaboration failures, including coordination breakdowns, constraint attribution errors, and recovery failures (Pan et al., 2025; Shao et al., 2024, 2026). \n\n**Preference Protocols and Evaluator Bias.** LLM-based judges are widely used as proxies for human feedback and as reward sources in evaluation and agent training. Prior work suggests that pairwise comparison can better align LLM evaluators with human judgment than scalar rating in some subjective settings (Liu et al., 2024b). At the same time, other studies show that preference judgments can be sensitive to presentation effects and distractors, motivating careful protocol design (Tripathi et al., 2025). These findings motivate \n\ntwo design choices in Plan-RewardBench: (i) we adopt a pairwise protocol aligned with trajectoryranking and preference-learning use cases, and (ii) we explicitly control for superficial cues in hard negatives (length/format), together with order-swap evaluation for pairwise-input LLM judges to mitigate positional bias. \n\n## **3 Benchmark Construction** \n\n## **3.1 Task setup** \n\nWe cast Plan-RewardBench as a pairwise trajectory preference task. Each example provides a tool environment _T_ (tool names, descriptions, and schemas), a multi-turn user interaction specifying the task, and two candidate trajectories ( _\u03c4A, \u03c4B_ ) consisting of assistant messages interleaved with tool calls and tool responses. The gold label indicates which trajectory is preferred under bucket-specific criteria, such as planning quality, tool grounding, recovery behavior, and refusal quality. We use a pairwise protocol because it supports (i) DRMs/GRMs training from trajectory preferences, (ii) inferencetime best-of- _N_ reranking and verifier-style filter- \n\ning over multiple sampled trajectories, and (iii) preference-based optimization such as DPO-style training. The same examples also support pointwise scoring: DRMs score each trajectory independently, and the higher-scoring one is selected. \n\n## **3.2 Data Source** \n\nThe overall construction pipeline is illustrated in Figure 1. We start from Toucan (Xu et al., 2025), which provides realistic MCP tool registries and executed tool responses. While Toucan trajectories serve as high-quality seeds, Plan-RewardBench is constructed from an expanded candidate pool per task to capture realistic success and failure modes. \n\nConcretely, for each selected (task, tool environment) instance, we re-execute agent rollouts using two agent runtimes\u2014 **Qwen-Agent** and **OpenAIAgent** to obtain full trajectories with tool calls and tool responses. We generate multiple rollouts per instance by varying the underlying base model, system prompts, temperatures/seeds, and (when applicable) tool-use policies. This multi-sampling process is a key source of natural diversity: it produces both strong solutions and naturally occurring mistakes under the same environment, which later enables controlled pairwise construction. As shown in Figure 2, our final dataset composition is dominated by natural rollouts (70%) to preserve realistic distribution, while strategically reinforced with perturbations (22%) and rule-based injections (8%) to broaden the spectrum of hard negatives. We intentionally do not force a uniform distribution over scenario families or source types: over-balancing rare families, especially Safety Refusal, tends to introduce low-signal or artifact-prone negatives. Instead, we prioritize label reliability and realistic failure coverage, and report per-family statistics and agreement separately. We then apply lightweight sanity checks to remove malformed traces (e.g., invalid tool-call formatting), hard execution failures, and degenerate cases, and compute basic features (total tokens, tool-token ratio, number of turns/calls) for stratified analysis. \n\n## **3.3 Scenario Families and Construction** \n\nWe bucket each instance into one of four scenario families, and design hard negatives to reflect that family\u2019s dominant failure patterns. \n\n**Safety Refusal.** We treat refusal as a trajectorylevel safety decision problem where the worst unsafe episode determines the final label. We annotate error episodes and score whether the agent detects, diagnoses, and recovers with meaningful changes (e.g., diagnose-and-fix or strategy-shift). Any hallucinated tool use or fabricated results contradicting tool outputs is treated as a critical failure. Hard negatives are constructed to cover blind-retry loops, confused diagnosis, premature surrender, and fabrication. \n\n## **3.4 Hard-Negative Construction** \n\nA core goal of Plan-RewardBench is to include confusable negatives that cannot be rejected by superficial cues (e.g., verbosity or formatting). We construct negatives from three complementary sources. \n\n**Natural negatives** come from the multi-model rollout pool (generated by diverse models such as Qwen-Plus and DeepSeek-R1; see Appendix A), capturing realistic agent mistakes (e.g., wrong tool choice, stale plans). **Rule-based perturbations** introduce controlled, reproducible failures, including constraint drop/swap, dependency corruption, realistic numeric or budget mistakes, entity substitution, unavailable-tool hallucination, unnecessary tool calls, and blind retries after tool errors. Finally, **minimal-edit perturbations** generate near-miss trajectories by starting from top-scoring candidates and applying small edits to assistant-visible text while preserving the recorded tool calls and outputs. This targets one intended degradation at a time, such as weaker alternatives, stale constraints, or shallower recovery reasoning. This \u201chigh-scoreto-near-miss\u201d design yields hard negatives that remain stylistically plausible while violating specific criteria. Figure 3 summarizes the composition of rejected trajectories by source across families. Appendix B gives additional templates, discard crite- \n\nria, and turn-count analysis. \n\n## **3.5 Preference Labeling and Pairwise Assembly** \n\nWe label trajectory preferences using a two-stage LLM-based protocol with additional human validation. First, we score each candidate with a **multi-judge panel** under family-specific rubrics (1\u20135 scale), producing scalar scores and diagnostic labels. For each trajectory, _K_ = 3 judges are aggregated by median score and majority-vote diagnostic tags. We run a separate **meta-review** pass when the score range is large (max _\u2212_ min _\u2265_ 2), when critical tags conflict (e.g., safety or tool-grounding violations), or when candidate scores tie; cases that remain ambiguous after meta-review are discarded. To reduce generator\u2013judge leakage, the generator set is disjoint from the judge set; the full model roster is provided in Appendix A. \n\nGiven a candidate set _{\u03c4i}_ , we aggregate judge scores robustly and assemble preference pairs with two constraints. **Difficulty control:** We pair a strong trajectory as _Chosen_ (high score; passes critical constraints) with a _Rejected_ trajectory drawn from lower-ranked candidates. We balance score gaps to include both near-miss comparisons (HardPair: median-score gap = 1) and discriminative pairs (EasyPair: gap _\u2265_ 2). This pair separability split is distinct from the trajectory-complexity Easy/Hard buckets used for planning scenarios, which are based on horizon/turn count and constraint complexity. **Bias control:** We stratify pair selection by coarse complexity signals (length, tooltoken ratio) and explicitly include style-controlled hard negatives so that preferences cannot be trivially exploited by superficial cues. \n\nFinally, we apply an independent post-check: a separate pairwise judge pass that directly compares the chosen and rejected trajectories to confirm the preference direction; only consistent pairs are retained. \n\n|**Scenario family**|**#Audited**|**Cohen\u2019s**_\u03ba_|\n|---|---|---|\n|Safety Refusal|51|0.86|\n|Tool-Irrelevance / Unavail.|80|0.81|\n|Robust Error Recovery|80|0.75|\n|Complex Planning|80|0.71|\n\n\n\nTable 2: **Human audit agreement.** Two annotators independently choose the preferred trajectory on a stratified subset of pairs following our bucket-specific rubrics. Two annotators independently choose the preferred trajectory for each audited pair. Disagreements are resolved by a third senior annotator using the same rubric. As shown in Table 2, pre-adjudication interannotator agreement (Cohen\u2019s _\u03ba_ ) is _substantial_ to _almost perfect_ ( _\u03ba \u2208_ [0 _._ 71 _,_ 0 _._ 86]) (Landis and Koch, 1977; Artstein and Poesio, 2008), confirming that the labels are reliable for evaluating trajectory-level judges. \n\n## **3.6 Dataset Statistics** \n\nTable 3 reports the composition and length statistics of Plan-RewardBench. We report the shared tool environment context length separately from the trajectory content. The trajectory length (dialogue + tool calls + outputs) varies across candidates and constitutes the primary source of complexity. Turn counts span 2\u201364 turns (mean 10.6, median 9.0), with Tool-Irrelevance concentrated in short interactions and Complex Planning / Robust Recovery covering longer horizons. Appendix B.2 reports turn-count statistics and performance-by-turn bins. \n\n## **4 Evaluation Results** \n\n**Evaluators and Protocols.** We evaluate three distinct families of evaluators on Plan-RewardBench. (i) **Discriminative Reward Models (DRMs)** typically process inputs in a pointwise manner, assigning a scalar score to each trajectory independently (\u25cb **r** ). (ii) **Generative Reward Models (GRMs)** are specialized models trained to output preferences, often generating a reasoning trace before the final decision. We evaluate them using the same pairwise protocol as LLM judges (\ufffd \u2713 _\u2662_ ). (iii) **General LLM Judges** employ a standard pairwise \u201cjudge-and-critique\u201d protocol. They receive the full context and both trajectories simultaneously, outputting a decision with a rationale. This \n\nmirrors standard practices in agentic data curation. \n\n**Input Representation.** To ensure fair comparison, all evaluators receive identical contexts: the TOOL-ENV registry (schema definitions), the multiturn conversation history, and the full interleaved trajectory (thoughts, tool calls, and execution outputs). Crucially, within each pairwise instance, the environment state and user intent are fixed; the only variable is the agent\u2019s trajectory. This isolation ensures that the evaluation strictly measures the judge\u2019s ability to discern _execution quality_ \u2014such as planning logic and safety adherence\u2014 rather than their ability to handle diverse prompt formats. Detailed bucket-specific prompts (e.g., for _Safety Refusal_ vs. _Complex Planning_ ) are provided in Appendix C. \n\n**Metrics and Bias Control.** Our primary metric is pairwise accuracy against gold labels. We report the overall performance (Avg) as the macroaverage across all seven evaluation splits presented in Table 4. This metric intentionally places greater emphasis on the _Complex Planning_ family (comprising four distinct splits based on horizon and difficulty), reflecting the benchmark\u2019s primary focus on agentic planning capabilities, while still incorporating Safety, Robustness, and Tool-Irrelevance as critical distinct dimensions. To mitigate positional bias in pairwise judges (GRMs and LLMs), we enforce an A/B swap protocol: each pair is evaluated twice with the order of candidates reversed, and we report the average correctness. For pointwise DRMs, position bias is inherently absent as candidates are scored independently. \n\n**Main Results and Observations.** Table 4 presents the pairwise accuracy of all evaluated models. To facilitate a fine-grained diagnosis of planning logic, we report the _Complex Planning_ scenario across four stratifications based on horizon (Single- vs. Multi-turn) and difficulty (Easy vs. Hard), alongside the aggregated scores for _Robust Recovery_ , _Safety Refusal_ , and _Tool Irrelevance_ . Overall, Plan-RewardBench proves to be a rigorous test bed: performance varies significantly across tasks, and no single evaluator consistently dominates every category. Accuracy remains relatively stable for short contexts ( _<_ 4k tokens), but drops sharply in the long-horizon regime ( _>_ 32k), where several evaluators fall below random chance. Pairwise LLM judges (Fig. 4a) exhibit the steepest collapse, likely because concatenating two full trajectories doubles the context load; pointwise RMs (Fig. 4b) show a smoother decay. Turn-count analysis in Appendix B.2 further shows a ShortHard, Medium-Peak, Long-Tail-Drop pattern and a widened 31+ turn gap between LLM judges and scalar RMs. \n\n## **5.2 Qualitative Failure Modes** \n\nBeyond aggregate metrics, we conduct a finegrained error analysis to categorize _why_ judges fail. We highlight four recurrent failure patterns below; \n\n**Misaligned Safety Priorities.** In _Safety Refusal_ , evaluators exhibit two critical flaws. First, they prefer brittle capability-based excuses (e.g., \u201cI lack tools\u201d) over robust policy-based refusals, mistakenly rewarding technical limitations as \u201ctransparency.\u201d Second, we observe compliance inertia in long-horizon episodes: judges often fail to penalize a final safety violation if it follows a sequence of successful benign turns, effectively allowing accumulated helpfulness to mask tail risks. \n\n**Blindness to Stale Constraints (Planning).** A frequent planning failure is the inability to track dynamic constraint updates. This suggests a bias where completed tool calls are rewarded regardless of their relevance to the updated context. \n\n**The \u201cEffort Bias\u201d (Irrelevance).** In _ToolIrrelevance_ scenarios, judges often penalize efficient, direct answers and instead reward agents that make unnecessary tool calls. This reflects a learned heuristic that \u201cmore tool usage equals more helpfulness,\u201d creating a vulnerability where agents can game the reward model by invoking irrelevant tools to pad their trajectories. \n\n**Superficial Recovery (Robustness).** In _Error Recovery_ , evaluators struggle to distinguish between blind retries (repeating the exact failed call) and intelligent corrections. Judges tend to reward the mere presence of a retry attempt-even if logically futile-indicating a lack of deep semantic understanding of execution error messages. \n\n## **6 Discussion and Conclusion** \n\n**Discussion.** While existing benchmarks evaluate tool-call correctness in multi-turn settings (Patil et al., 2025; Agarwal et al., 2025a), they often overlook broader semantic failures in planning and grounding. Plan-RewardBench bridges this gap by targeting trajectory-level logic rather than atomic execution. Our findings have critical implications for Agentic RL, where general LLMs are frequently deployed as scalar reward providers. We observe that even strong models struggle to distinguish high-quality execution from plausible \u201cnear-misses\u201d in our pairwise protocols. This suggests that their utility as absolute, pointwise judges in RL loops is likely fragile, prone to noise and biases (e.g., favoring verbosity) without the comparative context provided by pairwise evaluation. \n\n**Conclusion.** In this work, we introduced PlanRewardBench, a comprehensive suite for evaluating trajectory-level preference judgment. The release will include recorded offline trajectory pairs, tool registries, prompts/configs, and evaluation scripts; no tool re-execution or externalservice access is required. \n\n## **Limitations** \n\nGold labels for complex planning can contain some subjectivity, and MCP-style tool registries may not cover all proprietary APIs. Our scenario distribution is intentionally non-uniform: Safety Refusal is smaller because high-quality refusal hard negatives are rarer, although this family shows the highest human agreement. The current release focuses on English, text-based tool traces; multimodal and multi-agent settings remain important extensions. \n\n## **Ethical Considerations** \n\nPlan-RewardBench includes simulated _Safety Refusal_ requests for evaluation and alignment research only; we filter out actionable exploits and \n\ncaution against misuse for bypassing safety filters. AND TURN ANALYSIS** \n\n## **B.1 Additional Hard-negative Templates** \n\nRule-based perturbations use deterministic edits with family-specific validity checks. For Complex Planning, templates include constraint drop/swap (budget, time, or ordering), dependency corruption, arithmetic/budget mistakes, and near-neighbor entity substitution. For Tool-Irrelevance / Unavailability, templates inject unnecessary tool calls, unsupported capability claims, or tool/result hallucinations while preserving the original tool registry. For Robust Error Recovery, templates create blind retries, premature surrender, or confused diagnoses after explicit tool errors. For Safety Refusal, templates create late refusal, unsafe compliance, or weaker capability-based refusals in long benignprefix contexts. All edited candidates are discarded if the edited text contradicts recorded tool outputs in an unintended way, changes the user request, or introduces formatting/length artifacts that would make the pair trivial. \n\nMinimal-edit perturbations are generated from \n\nhigh-scoring trajectories under constraints: preserve the original tool calls and tool responses, alter only assistant-visible text, change one target failure factor, and keep the surface style comparable to the source trajectory.
